summary: Glenwood Springs' affordable-housing program is anchored by Ballot Measure 2C (Nov 2022), a 2.5% accommodations tax dedicated to a city Workforce Housing Fund projected at ~$1.7M/year. The fund has backstopped major LIHTC awards (The Benedict 2023 R1; Canyon Vista / Glenwood Gardens 2024 R2) and capitalized two Habitat partnerships on city-owned land.

paragraphs.text: On November 8, 2022, Glenwood Springs voters passed Ballot Measure 2C by a margin of 1,948-1,611, establishing a 2.5% accommodations (lodging and short-term rental) tax dedicated to a city Workforce Housing Fund. City Council referred the measure on September 1, 2022; the question forecasts approximately $1.7M/year in dedicated revenue.

The fund is governed by a city Workforce Housing Fund Advisory Board (WHFAB), with Council appropriation authority and an annual audit; uses are restricted to housing for persons working within city limits or the 81601 ZIP code.

Recent 2C appropriations include roughly $2.3M to the Canyon Vista project across 2024-2025 plus a separately approved $730,640 in fee coverage, a $3.8M package approved in December 2024 (described by Council as the largest single affordable-housing investment in the city's history), a $3M commitment ($1.5M of 2C plus a $1.5M general-fund loan) to a Habitat condo-conversion project in exchange for 15 priority units, and $300,000 ($100K/year, 2025-2027) to the regional Good Deeds buy-down program.

The Benedict (2700 / 2800 Midland Ave) received a 9% federal Housing Tax Credit award of $1,449,710 in CHFA's 2023 Round One announcement. Developer: Archdiocesan Housing, Inc. (Catholic Charities Housing). Program: 34 one-bedroom apartments for seniors aged 55+. The Garfield County Housing Authority committed seven project-based vouchers to the project.

Glenwood Gardens / Canyon Vista (51993 U.S. Highway 6) received federal 4% Housing Tax Credits plus state Housing Tax Credits in CHFA's 2024 Round Two announcement (November 27, 2024). Developer: Cohen-Esrey Development Group. State credit amount: $2,127,517. Program: 80 apartments serving 20%-80% AMI. The project is marketed under both names — CHFA's award report uses 'Glenwood Gardens'; local press and 2025 council documents use 'Canyon Vista.'

CHFA's 2025 Round One announcement (May 9, 2025) included a Glenwood Springs project in the awardee list, continuing the pipeline; the November 2025 Round Two announcement did not include a city project.

City Council adopted the Strategic Housing Plan Update in July 2023. The plan identifies that approximately 1,491 of Glenwood households (about 38%) are housing-cost-burdened. To halve cost burden, fill all unfilled jobs, and reduce the in-commute share from 78% to 70%, the plan finds the city needed 1,346 new units in 2023, rising to 1,858 units by 2043.

The plan's implementation strategy includes a 3% affordable-housing-stock annual growth target (aligned with Colorado Proposition 123 compliance) and a commitment to hire a dedicated city Housing Manager.

sections.heading: City-owned land disposition for affordable housing

paragraphs.text: A 2020 city analysis identified the 8th & Midland Avenue and Airport Road (across from Cardiff Glen) parcels as priority sites for affordable housing. An RFP process launched June 2021 with a final RFP issued January 2022; Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley was the sole respondent. The award produced two projects: 14 three-bedroom townhomes on Airport Road and a smaller condo build at 8th & Midland (later scaled and renamed 'The Confluence,' partnered with Vederra Modular).

Council is now pursuing roughly 100-150 workforce units at the Vogelaar Park site (8th & School). The concept programs 52 apartments, 8 fourplexes, 26 townhouses, and 64 micro-units, supported by a DOLA 'More Housing Now / Land Use Initiative' planning grant. The county commission issued a letter of support for the city's $270K planning-study grant application.

Glenwood Springs is a charter member of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC), a 501(c)(3) coalition operating across the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys. Member jurisdictions pay approximately $20,000/year in operating dues; the 2025 coalition operating budget is around $180,000 across nine members. Glenwood Springs contributed $10,000 of seed money toward initial formation.

On Good Deeds capital specifically, the City committed $200,000 in the program's inaugural 2024 year and then $300,000 over 2025-2027 ($100,000/year, by a 6-1 council vote). In 2024 the Good Deeds program deed-restricted 8 homes across Garfield County, with 4 inside Glenwood city limits; the average buy-down was approximately $260,000 per home.

The Garfield County Housing Authority (GCHA) operates a project-based-voucher pipeline relevant to Glenwood-area projects: 7 PBVs committed at The Benedict and a planned 20 PBVs at Canyon Vista, alongside other county-wide allocations.

The county opted in to Colorado Proposition 123. The Colorado State Housing Board approved $1.3M of Prop 123 Concessionary Debt for Canyon Vista (part of a larger $19.6M / 9-project package) plus a $3.5M Transformational Housing Loan Fund award to Cohen-Esrey for the same project; the county also authorized a separate $4.1M CHFA bond issuance.
